The reason we're here together doing this now is for specifics. You can get the generalities of pitch anything, strong, the grand architecture, these things from the book. Then you can, from the book, you can intuit how to do these things. But we're here to get very, very specific and tools to make this happen. Let's get specific about the grand architecture. Step one is you have to build a great landing page for your deal. In our business, we call it a pitch deck. Part of it is it's some physical or web environment that people can learn about what you have, your deal, your product, your idea, whatever it is you're selling and pitching. Some of you may do that on landing pages on websites. We do it with a PDF called a pitch deck. I'll show you how we do it exactly in a minute. The elements that we have are these. One is an orientation to what the basic deal is. If you sell lawn mowers, in the orientation, you have to say it weighs 200 pounds. It has a blade that spins at 20,000 RPM and it can cut a quarter acre on a tank of gas. People will know it's a home-based product. Guys who have a farm with 100 square acres know not to buy this lawn mower. You have to orient people to what it is, who it's for. They don't waste time with you. One is the orientation. Number two is the big idea. You have to give people the big idea. We've done it in the Strong series before, but I'll review it for you. A big idea can be this. If you're able to build a semiconductor for cell phones such that when you walk into a building, an apartment, or an office complex and you don't lose signal, if you did that, then you would have billions of people interested in your product. Verizon, AT&T, and France Telecom would want to buy what you have. We in fact have that semiconductor and we've built it into the iPhone and the Blackberry and France Telecom and AT&T and Verizon have done contracts with us to buy it. We are working on growing to a billion dollars in revenue with our semiconductor company. That's a simple explanation of the big idea, a semiconductor that doesn't lose signal indoors. It's selling it to big wireless carriers. You have to give the big idea. In the landing page or the pitch deck, you also have to explain why now? Why not do this six months ago? Why wasn't this done two years ago? Why shouldn't the investor or buyer just wait and do it six months from now? Why do it now? Have to explain that. In the landing page or the pitch deck, you have to give clear economics. You the buyer pay this much. Over time, you make this much back. It costs you this much along the way and this is your return. You have to give clear economics. You have to give a clear value proposition. Who this is for? Why they should buy it? How it is used and how your life will be better after you buy it than it was before. Whether it's a jet fighter or a birthday cake. You have to give the value proposition. We give downside protection. Downside protection is if you buy the deal or the idea or the product, how you're protected from losing money. At least you won't lose money. If you do lose money, it would just be a little bit and not a lot. We always try on the landing page or the pitch deck to give real insight that shows this isn't generic. We know what we're doing. We're inside this market. We know the stuff better than anybody else. If you work with us, you can leverage the insight that we have. Let me take you through our pitch deck now and you can see exactly how we do these things. I'll now introduce you to Velocity. This is the method I developed as part of Intersection Capital, which is the firm I founded to help companies raise capital and sell themselves. Really, its job is to bring as many prospects as possible into phase one of the grand architecture. Again, if you recall, it's important to have as many people or many groups in phase one to work with. When you get to the closing table, you have options. Many people ask, how do you get the most amount of people into phase one of the grand architecture process? I'll show you now what we developed. This is a company that we're helping. As you can see, we've packaged them in a way that captures the big idea, in this case 187 words. I'll just go through it. Think for a moment about what happens when you get a wound. You expose the inside of your body to the outside world. What happens next? Germs, bacteria, and other microbes flood in. This is always bad news. Once an infection happens, your chances of getting very sick get worse minute by minute. Your body will start to fight against the infection. Your doctor will pitch in two more about this later, but there's one issue that could determine 80% of your success. Is the infection within a base of biofilm? Unfortunately named, biofilm is not actually good for you. It is the foundation for 80% of human wound infections. If you could address the biofilm problem, you would have a major opportunity to lower healthcare costs in the US and even save lives. This pitch deck is dedicated to the economic upside from solving the biofilm problem. Unlike an executive summary or a business plan or a PowerPoint presentation, what these velocity decks do, and I'm going to take you one through one in full, is capture the big idea. What's the essence of the company? Take you through another one now so you can see. This is a very interesting one. We're helping a company who has a completely altruistic purpose and they go in to the most of biodiverse ecosystems in the world and try and help the countries they're in save them. Here's the opportunity in 103 words. To save humanity, you have to tackle some really nasty problems. You already know about them. Climate change, clean energy, and species loss. To us, the problem of species loss stands out. Why? Because it's irreversible. Areas where there is high species concentration have become exceedingly rare and valuable. Out of 192 countries, only 17 make the watch list. None are in Europe. The US has won. The remaining 15 are in South America, Central America, Africa, and Asia. All are at risk. But many are difficult to reach. Others are dangerous to operate in once you reach them. Some are prohibitively expensive to deal with. Several are targeted by other organizations. But there are four countries with areas of biodiversity, wealth, and species concentration that we have identified as reachable, affordable, and savable. Our immediate focus is to defend these areas. This prospectus describes one of our priorities, how we selected it, and why it must be defended, and what it costs to do so. So again, this velocity deck conveys the big idea of what's going on here, what's important, and what happens if you succeed. These are incredibly important ways to help people understand that this deal is worth looking at. I'll show you another quick introduction. The big idea in 143 words, if you want to describe Western North Dakota in just a few words, you might say fast growing economy or beautiful country or nice people. But if you really wanted to capture what's going on, you could simply say no vacancy. Now this pitch deck and these big ideas are available in the resources section, and I recommend you look through them. Again, velocity is the way we bring the most amount of people to phase one of our process of the grand architecture. I'd like to show you a little bit more of the Chelexa deck so you can understand beyond the big idea how we show people and get about a deal and get them excited about the deal. So we tried to uncover that first of all it doesn't offend people and makes them say, what's inside of this? I want to look inside. Here you see a deal summary. It's very important to tell people what they're getting. Here you may not be in the deal business, but we say who the CEO is, what the product is, where they are, how much money they've raised so far, and what they need the money for and how big their market is. We orient people to the deal. So people looking at this can say, I'm interested or I'm not interested from these deal basics. Next we go into the big idea. I showed you this one already. It's very nice. What happens when you get a wound? You expose the inside of your body to the outside world. In that we show people that this is a disruptive technology and it's a big market. It's a big market and how much money is at stake. Moving forward we show people again quantify the size of the problem. Then we try and show some provocative information and some insight about the market. Here we say it's the fourth leading cause of death. Most people don't know that even sophisticated healthcare providers and many people don't know there are only three options to treating a hospital-gained infection. Remove it, kill it, or cover it with dressings. This is where we start to create some very simple understanding of the product. Next many times if we give information-rich pages then we try and provide some eye candy so people don't are not reading facts and information constantly. As we move through this deck we give people a chance to understand what the opportunity to improve on the current state of affairs is. Moving forward we show people that if you were an investor in this market how you could succeed with it. Of course we show the value proposition and exactly what the value of this product is. We show how the company goes to market and lastly we tell people who the team is and who is going to be executing. This is enough to get many companies interested, many people interested in looking at the deal and it fills the pipeline. Column 1, phase 1, the first phase of the grand architecture. I'll take you through one more quickly and then you can explore these yourself. You can explore these yourself in the resources section. So again here's nature culture. We showed you the big idea to save humanity. You have to tackle some really nasty problems as we said before. Here again you'll start to see it repeated. You can orient yourself to the project by the project summary. We give eye candy, gives people a break from reading factual information. Then we tell people what the problem is and do a little intrigue. It's not what you think. After the problem we give the solution. Following that we show exactly how the business model works, a little bit more eye candy. Then we like to give a clear definition of exactly what it is. We give some more detail because in this kind of business you have to tell people exactly the locations you're trying to save, more eye candy and because this is a business that does pulls it to heartstrings you can show even more eye candy. This is one of my favorite pages, the Jaguar. We show the management team and that's it. You have now seen a full velocity deck. Again, this is in the resources section. I encourage you to go look at that. My final thoughts on this, whether you have a landing page, you have a pitch deck or a product brochure, it needs to be as compelling as possible to get many, many people into phase one of the grand architecture. As you can see, super important to build a great pitch deck or landing page to bring as many people into your phase one of your pitch process. That's step one, have to do that. Step two is you have to have a great strong pitch. Strong set the frame, tell the story, reveal the intrigue, offer the prize, nail the hook point, get the deal strong. You have to have a great pitch. This is the pitch that takes into account using time frames to your benefit. When you have other people trying to time constrain you, you know how to deal with it. Prizing yourself. Even though you're the seller and you're offering the deal, other people want to get in. They're asking to get into your deal. I'm the prize. You're the commodity. You try and win my attention even though I'm the seller. You're setting time frames. You're using prize frames. You recognize analyst frames when somebody's trying to derail your presentation or your pitch by being overly analytical. You know how to deal with that. Close distance on it, shut it down. You recognize the power frame when people are using their power and their status and their authority and their celebrity and their influence and their money to try and get concessions from you to make you economically weak. You recognize that power frame and you know how to again close distance on it, deal with it, shut it down. The strong pitch does all these things. You know how to put your deal in context of changing forces to give your big idea to manage time frames, manage attention, create tension when people aren't paying quite enough attention to you to put counter pressure on them, to make them qualify themselves back to you. It's strong. It does all these things. You'll know how to do this. You go through it here in Pitch Mastery. That's step two is to build your great pitch. What this is all about. Step three, you've got to use your pitch deck or your landing page and your great strong pitch together to just attract as many potential deals or buyers or JV partners or investors get attract as many as possible into phase one. You're really, really going to do that by proliferating the landing page or the pitch deck out into the market as far and as wide as you can get. Bring it as many people and then use your pitch to get them really into phase one where they want what you have and you start working with them. Step four, you have to know what your transition actions are between phase one, phase two and phase three which is closing. I can tell you what ours are. They're probably very similar to yours the way it is in your business. I'll tell you what ours are in my business. To go from phase one to phase two, we need what's called an expression of interest. Sometimes an expression of interest is, Orin, I love you and I'm going to do this deal with you. Sometimes it's a term sheet but it's something that is solid. It is either the expression of interest verbally or it is a physical document but it says we plan to advance. We don't know if we're going to close and we don't know everything about you. We haven't done due diligence yet but based on what we know, we like the pricing roughly. We like the delivery times roughly. We like the value proposition. We like the economics. We like who you are and we want it. Moving from phase one to phase two is about wanting. For me, I can't move somebody from phase one to phase two unless they've expressed interest either verbally or physically in a document. I'm never going to move somebody into phase two out of phase one unless they said I want it. Somebody's in phase one and they say, I'm kind of interested. I'll give you a term sheet. That is not enough to move them into phase two. I say, stop. You stay there. You cannot advance until you say, Orin, I love you. I am going to do this if it all comes true, the things you said. You work out and do diligence. Move somebody from phase one to phase two. You need a transition point. You need something to happen. To move people from phase two into phase three. Now you need something more solid. It's not an expression of interest. For us, it's a purchase and sale agreement. For you, it might be a draft purchase order. It might be a contract. It might be a very strong verbal agreement. It might be a letter of interest, but it's not the closing document, but it's something that indicates very strong intent to move forward. See wanting escalate higher. Now we're in phase three, which is really just prior to closing. To get some from phase three into closing, for us, we need them to complete all of their due diligence. No more questions. They've looked at every document. They've looked at every technical detail they want to look at. They've asked every question. No more questions. We're through due diligence. There's a setting of a closing date. No one is allowed to move out of phase three. You see my language? I allow people to move out of phase three to buy something only when they've met my requirements. They have completed due diligence. No more questions. There's a setting of a closing date. Now you can come out of phase three and go into closing. In a car sale, all of this might happen by the end of the day. Review sale, those giant earth-moving caterpillar equipments, tractors, maybe it would be a month to do all this. For a screenplay, I know some screenwriters, and maybe this takes six months or a year. Phase one, phase two, phase three might take a year for a screenwriter, but he still has to do all these same things. Get as many people into phase one with the landing page. Use what indicates a transition point. Get people transitioned into phase two. Get a stronger commitment. Transition people into phase three. Pick a closing date and make sure there's no more questions, and then get out of phase three into closing. No matter what you have, you have to go through this process. In an online world, phase one is an opt-in, right? Phase two is a live webinar, and phase three is the clicking of the order button. We all, whether we're online or we sell caterpillar tractors or screenplays or large companies, we all have to go through this grand architecture. The focus of this training is the strong pitch, but you have to know where you are in the grand architecture in order to just pitch effectively, right? Number one, for sure. You have to know where you are in the grand architecture to pitch effectively, because it's very rare to have somebody who can do a one and done pitch. They pitch one person perfectly, that person buys, and the whole process is over. Hardly ever happens. Typically, your pitch will get better and better and better over time with the more humans you engage with it, right? So one is you bring lots of people into the grand architecture so you can pitch many times and get your pitch refined until it's actually working. A second reason you need the grand architecture is what we started talking about, and the whole reason it actually came into existence. You have to eradicate your own neediness, no matter how good you are, right? And I'm pretty damn good, right? Maybe as good as it gets. If you have a big deal you're trying to close, right? There's high stakes, and it's likely that you're going to act needy, right? If you do not have a few options of people to close with, right, you will act needy. Now, let me make this real for you. Imagine you're selling a house. There's only one buyer that wants to move forward, and you go into the final negotiations on price. The buyer says, we thought about it, and we want you to replace the roof, right? Or we won't go forward. You want me to replace the roof, why? Because we want you to do it or we're not going to close. What do you do, right? If you're like most people, you concede and you lose $15,000, or however much that costs. However, if you have three very motivated buyers, and one of them says, hey, we want you to replace the roof, right? What do you do? You say, go kick rocks. I'm not replacing the roof. You want a new roof, you buy it. These other two guys not getting a new roof for free, and you're not getting it either. So decide if you want this house, because if you want it with me putting a new roof on it, you're not getting it, right? And you say it that way because you're not needy. So in one case, you have no other buyers, and you say, I'll be glad to put a new roof on it for you. In the other case, you have a couple buyers, and you say, you don't get a roof. And if I might just kick you out for being so rude to me, never going to get this house. The difference between one and the other is running good grand architecture. The third reason you need this, and I'm pretty convinced of this, it's important to be in a process. When you're doing strong or when you're doing tactics, when you're focused on the nitty-gritty of pitching and selling and explaining your idea, things are constantly changing around. Your circumstances are changing, and you're adjusting to them. You don't know where you are in the larger process. The grand architecture gives you a mission. You have objectives you have to reach, and you have goals. Now tactics, strong in the pitching, support the mission and the objectives. But you have to know where you are in the big picture. Have you ever been deep in a project and gotten that fear in the pit of your stomach that it's not going to be good? You're halfway through it, and you're like, the book isn't going to be good, or the design we're making on, or the video we're working on, or the semiconductor we're designing. I'm not sure it's going to be good, or I don't know if it's going to deliver on time, or maybe it will be good, but it will just be irrelevant. Nobody will want it anymore. You get this fear, like, I don't know where I am. I'm lost. I know I'm doing good work, but maybe this work is meaningless in the greater scheme of things. Then one of your partners, your boss, or your partner, or someone you work with who's holding the big picture in their mind, who's not down in the weeds with you, came in and they reoriented you to the process of why you're doing this thing, and how it's certain to be good and reconfirmed with you that you're in the right place at the right time doing the right thing. The grand architecture, the way I've designed it, is your tool for doing the same thing. It's when you get down in the weeds and you get lost. It's your way of knowing I'm doing the right thing and everything's going to be okay. I'm in good process. The grand architecture lets you say to yourself, or to you and your partner, I am in good process, and I don't lose sight of the big picture, which is get to the closing table with some options. It's important to visualize the grand architecture. This is the process you'll be running, and you'll, of course, be using strong when you pitch any individual person or group, but you have to understand how to run good process. Good process is what an investment bank or an investment advisor would do for you, and its job is to help create as many closing options as possible for you so you're not needy at the closing table. The grand architecture happens in three main phases. What you'll do is you'll start with phase one in which you initialize the deal. You will take your deal book or your pitch book as in my business. This may be your landing page in your business or even a product brochure, and you'll show it to as big an audience as possible. You'll do that to solicit their interest. Some people will become very interested in your deal, and your deal will be initialized. You put those people in phase one. This is where you'll use the strong method. Set the frame, tell the story, reveal the intrigue, offer the prize, and start to try and nail the hook point. You'll work with these people and pitch them as well as can be done. Someone in phase one will say, I'm ready to buy, and you will say, no. I'm not going to go outside my process. You won't rush them to the closing table because it's likely at the closing table if you have no options, they will retrade you on price. They'll ask for price concessions because they will sense that you have no options. This process is about creating options. You'll use strong to season these people and move as many of them as possible up to this closing line. Rather than letting anybody rush quickly into phase two, you're going to work with them and get to a point of advancement. A point of advancement is you're going to ask them to signal some desire to move forward. Not everyone will move forward. Some will fall off and you have to be okay with that. You're going to say, Bob, Mary, Susan, Group, I need you to specifically tell me that you're ready to move forward. You're interested in the product. You're interested in the deal. Some will come with you. Now you'll have a little fewer amount of people to work with, but they will be somewhat seasoned. At this point, you will continue to work with them, season them, try to advance them in the deal. If anyone tries to say, hey, I want to rush off and close with you, you're going to say, no, that's not my process. We're just in phase two of the process. You're going to move them up to the close to the edge here. You're going to look for an expression of interest. In my business, there might be a letter of intent, a memorandum of understanding, or even a contract. In your business, it might be a purchase order. You'll say, hey, if you want to go further in this deal at this point, you need to issue a purchase, a draft purchase order, or you need to put a draft contract in place. If you don't do that by Friday or by the 15th or in two weeks, whatever it is, then you're not going to be able to go forward in the deal. You can say that because you have options. If anyone of these people say, I'm ready to close, you're going to say, no, we're not in phase three yet. Now you're going to move some of them forward with the expression of interest into phase three. Not all of them will come with you. Now, again, you're going to continue to season these guys, and now they'll be putting pressure on you, that we put in pressure on you to give concessions, but you're not going to go close any of them prematurely. You're going to move them to the edge just as we did in the other phases, and now you're going to look for a commitment to close. Commitment to close. This is what the Grand Architecture is all about, getting to the point where you have options to move into closing. Anyone of these individuals or groups says, we want to change terms, or we want to change price, or we want to price concession. You have strength because you have options. You say, no, I'm not giving a price concession, and if you don't want to move forward, I will close with someone else. Now you're going to apply that, and you will close with someone. If you sell products, you may close with multiple people. The deal business, typically you'll only have one buyer. This process will allow you to get to the closing process with options and close with one person. Let's review and take it from the beginning. If we clear the screen, the Grand Architecture, how to create as many closing options as possible. We start with a large population. We move some of them into phase one. Phase one is when we initialize the deal, and we have multiple interested parties. We don't take any party prematurely to the closing table. We bring them up to the edge, and then we look for a point of advancement. We get the point of advancement. We make them signal their desire to move forward, and then move into phase two. In phase two, we work to season the buyers and work with them a little bit. Again, if anyone buyer says I'm ready to close, you say, we're still in process. You move them up to the edge, and you really say, now I need another point of advancement or an expression of interest. This is something more significant than just a signal of desire. We're really looking for some kind of written expression of interest. It can be verbal in some businesses, but in my business, I really want someone to say, hey, here's a letter of intent. Here's a draft purchase order. You're going to move some of those people, and they're not into phase three. They're not all going to come with you. Then we're going to run the same process over again. You're going to have less people, but they're going to be very, very interested in season to the deal. We use strong to bring them up to the edge. They're on the edge. Now, we really have options going into closing. We're going to ask for commitment to close. If any one person tries to retrait us on price or terms, we're going to say, no, we have other options. We're going to close with someone else. Then we're going to go into closing with one individual. That's the grand architecture. It's incredibly important to run this good process. When I was raising $15 million for a project near Notre Dame University, I had a timeline of 35 days to raise all of the money, $15 million. 10 days into it, 10 days of 35 days, I had raised $1 million. Only $1 million. I started freaking out. I was sure it could not be done. I was becoming dejected because I knew if I did not raise $15 million in 35 days, we would lose a million dollars of our own money, the deposit we put up. Now I started going beyond the freak out mode. I was going almost to the point of no return where I was scared and scared as needy. My partner, John, brought me a spreadsheet and was the breakdown of all of our last 10 deals. Every single one of them tracked to this exact same curve. A third of the way in, we had only raised a little bit of the money. We always got to the end with all the money. This was my introduction to the grand architecture and its power to calm you down, to orient yourself in space and time and to allow you to work with poise and strength and confidence. Grand pitch mastery is a grand architecture worksheet. You and your business partners will use this to be able to focus on giving great strong pitches, the tactics, and keep yourself aware of where you are in the grand process and how to get to the close without getting needy or nervous or freaking out. Most importantly though, you will know not to be tempted when one prospect in phase one raises his arm and says, I'm ready to close because you know that if you rush into closing with that guy, he will retrade you at the closing table. When he raises his arm prematurely and says, I'm ready to close, you say, sit down. You're in my grand architecture. You play by my rules. You stay in my process. When we're ready, we'll let you know when you can buy and what you have to pay. That's what the grand architecture does for you because if anybody rushes to the closing, you know it will be bad for you. You bring everybody along so you have options. Let's you keep those options. It's the grand architecture that allows you to do this.
